aspects.name: 2D area and perimeter
aspects.description: Student is given dimensions (or a target area/perimeter) of a planar figure — rectangle, square, triangle, or circle — and must apply the standard 2D formula (A=lw, A=s^2, A=(1/2)bh, A=pi*r^2, perimeter/circumference) in either the forward direction (dimensions -> measurement) or reverse direction (measurement + one dimension -> other dimension). The cognitive operation is direct formula recall and one-step arithmetic substitution, with no scaling, no compositing, and no 3D solids involved.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A1 differs from A2 because A2's figures are 3D solids requiring volume or surface-area formulas; differs from A3 because A1 never involves comparing two similar figures or applying a side-ratio -> area-ratio relation; differs from A4 because A1's figure is a single named primitive given dimensionally in the stem, not built from a context, composite, or coordinate description.

types.name: Rectangle / square area or perimeter
types.description: A rectangle or square is described by side length(s); the student computes area or perimeter (forward) OR is given the area/perimeter plus one side and finds the missing side (reverse).
types.core_skill_demand: Recall A=lw or A=s^2 (and P=2(l+w) or P=4s) and either multiply two integers or divide the given area by the known side to find the missing one.

characteristics: Both sides are small whole numbers given explicitly in the stem (e.g. length 7, width 6)
Single direct application of A=lw, A=s^2, P=2(l+w), or P=4s
Reverse questions require exactly one integer division (area/known side, perimeter/4) with integer result
easy.example_stem: What is the area, in square inches, of a rectangle with a length of 7 inches and a width of 6 inches?

characteristics: One side described indirectly via a one-step relation (e.g. 'perimeter of square Y is 2 times the perimeter of square X') so the student must first solve a small linear step before applying the formula
OR the stem asks for the symbolic expression that gives the area rather than its numeric value, requiring choice between multiplicative vs additive forms
Numbers may be larger (two-digit) but still integer
medium.example_stem: Square X has a side length of 12 cm. The perimeter of square Y is 2 times the perimeter of square X. What is the length of one side of square Y?

characteristics: Not represented at Hard for this type; rectangle/square forward and reverse questions remain at Easy or Medium tier in the bank.

must_preserve: Single named primitive (rectangle or square) given by sides or by an area/perimeter measure
Exactly one formula application; reverse versions require exactly one inverse operation
Answer is a whole number (or simple terminating decimal) in the same unit class as the stem
must_vary: Which measurement is asked (area vs perimeter, forward vs reverse)
Unit (inches, centimeters, meters, units)
Specific integers for length/width
MCQ distractors that correspond to confusing perimeter vs area (e.g. 2(l+w) vs lw), or forgetting to square for area-of-square
common_bugs: Cloner swaps area and perimeter formulas, producing distractors that are no longer plausible (e.g. ignoring the 2(l+w) vs lw trap)
Reverse clone yields a non-integer side length, breaking SPR or making the MCQ key irrational
Adds a second relation step at Easy level (e.g. 'length is 5 more than twice the width'), drifting from Easy to Medium difficulty

types.name: Triangle area or side from perimeter
types.description: A triangle is given either (a) with base and height so the student computes A=(1/2)bh, or (b) with two sides and a perimeter so the student finds the third side by subtraction.
types.core_skill_demand: Recall A=(1/2)bh and multiply, OR recall perimeter = sum of sides and subtract. At Hard, the same formula is applied but base/height must first be extracted from radical side lengths or from coordinates.

characteristics: Either base+height with small integers OR perimeter+two-sides with small integers
All numeric values are integers and the answer is an integer
No figure interpretation beyond labeling sides
easy.example_stem: A triangle has a base length of 40 cm and a height of 90 cm. What is the area, in sq cm, of the triangle?

characteristics: Base and height are larger integers, OR the height is given indirectly (e.g. the legs of a right triangle, requiring recognition that the two legs serve as base and height)
Answer may be a non-integer simple decimal or fraction (e.g. 7.5 = 15/2)
Stem may include a not-to-scale figure
medium.example_stem: The figure shows the lengths, in inches, of two sides of a right triangle (legs 3 and 5). What is the area of the triangle?

characteristics: Side lengths are given as radicals (e.g. 2*sqrt(2), sqrt(80)) and student must identify the two legs of the right triangle and multiply radical expressions
OR the triangle is embedded in the coordinate plane and one leg's length must be computed via the distance formula before applying A=(1/2)bh
Multi-step: identify-which-sides-are-legs -> compute -> apply formula
hard.example_stem: A right triangle has sides 2*sqrt(2), 6*sqrt(2), and sqrt(80). What is the area of the triangle?

must_preserve: Triangle stays a single triangle (not compounded with another shape)
The required formula is A=(1/2)bh or sum-of-sides-equals-perimeter; no introduction of trigonometry or Heron's formula
At Hard, the right-triangle structure (legs perpendicular) must remain so that base and height are unambiguous
must_vary: Whether the question is forward area or reverse missing-side-of-perimeter
Specific integer or radical values
Orientation of the figure if one is supplied
common_bugs: Cloner drops the (1/2) factor in distractors, making the wrong-answer pattern transparent
Hard clone replaces radicals with integers, collapsing the Hard variant back to Easy/Medium
Coordinate-leg clone uses points that do not yield a clean integer or simple-radical leg length

types.name: Circle area / circumference / diameter
types.description: A circle is described by its radius, diameter, or circumference; the student applies A=pi*r^2 and C=2*pi*r (or d=2r) once, either forward or in reverse.
types.core_skill_demand: Recall and apply circle formulas. At Hard, the same recall is reused inside a ratio-of-areas comparison or radius-substitution context.

characteristics: Not represented at Easy in this bank — College Board treats single-circle formula application as Medium-tier.
Radius (or diameter, or circumference) given as a single integer or simple decimal
Answer expressed in terms of pi (e.g. '43 pi', '4.41') so no decimal approximation of pi is required
May ask for sum-of-two-circle-areas (still one formula applied twice) or to convert between r, d, and C
medium.example_stem: A circle has a radius of 43 meters. What is the area, in square meters, of the circle? -> 1,849 pi.

characteristics: Radii are given symbolically (e.g. radius 3n and 129n) and the student must compute the ratio of areas, recognizing (r2/r1)^2
OR multi-circle / multi-step reasoning where one circle's radius is itself derived from another quantity
hard.example_stem: Circle A has radius 3n and circle B has radius 129n. The area of B is how many times the area of A?

must_preserve: Single-circle formula application (or two parallel applications for sum-of-areas)
Answer expressible exactly in terms of pi or as the integer multiplier in 'k*pi' form
Forward/reverse direction stays consistent (don't swap which of r, d, C is the unknown without preserving difficulty)
must_vary: Which quantity is given vs unknown (r, d, C, A)
The numeric radius or diameter
Whether the answer is asked as 'k pi' (SPR-friendly) or in MCQ form
common_bugs: Cloner picks a radius whose square is not clean, producing ugly k pi values
Distractors confuse C=2 pi r with A=pi r^2 but in arithmetically obvious ways
Hard ratio-of-areas clone forgets that the area ratio is (r2/r1)^2, not r2/r1, so the labeled correct answer is itself wrong

aspects.name: 3D volume and surface area
aspects.description: Student is given the dimensions (or a target volume/surface area) of a 3D solid — right rectangular prism, cube, cylinder, cone, sphere, hemisphere, or square/triangular prism — and applies the standard 3D formula in the forward direction (dimensions -> V or SA) or in reverse (V or SA + some dimensions -> missing dimension or symbolic expression).
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A2 differs from A1 because the figure is a 3D solid (cube, prism, cylinder, cone, sphere); differs from A3 because no two similar solids are compared via a scale factor; differs from A4 because the solid is a single primitive named in the stem rather than a composite, scale-model, or context-described object.

types.name: Rectangular prism / cube volume or surface area
types.description: A right rectangular prism (sometimes a cube) is given by length, width, and height; the student computes the volume (V=lwh, V=s^3) or surface area, or reverses to find a missing edge.
types.core_skill_demand: Recall V=lwh / V=s^3 / SA=2(lw+lh+wh) / SA=6 s^2 and either multiply three numbers, divide the volume by the product of two dimensions, or apply two formulas in sequence (e.g. from V infer edge, then compute SA).

characteristics: All three dimensions given as small integers in the stem
Single application of V=lwh; the formula itself may even be quoted in the stem
Answer is a clean integer
easy.example_stem: A right rectangular prism has a length of 11 m, width 8 m, height 10 m. What is the volume?

characteristics: Cube volume from edge length (large integer, e.g. 41^3) OR finding the missing dimension when V and the area of the base are given
Surface-area variants where one face is missing (e.g. 'box without a lid' computes 5 s^2 not 6 s^2)
Numbers larger; the answer remains an integer
medium.example_stem: A cube has an edge length of 41 inches. What is the volume?

characteristics: Two-formula chain: e.g. given SA -> infer edge -> compute V (cube); given V -> infer edge -> compute SA
Symbolic answer expressing surface area as an expression in a variable (e.g. base area 24t cm^2, find SA in terms of t)
Surface area of a non-cube prism computed from scratch (three different face pairs)
hard.example_stem: A cube has surface area 54 sq m. What is the volume, in cubic meters?

must_preserve: Solid is a right rectangular prism (possibly a cube) — no oblique prisms, no compound solids
Number of unknowns matches difficulty (1 unknown at Easy, 1 plus a formula chain at Hard)
Surface-area formula matches the stated geometry (e.g. 5-face 'without lid' is preserved as 5-face, not silently treated as 6-face)
must_vary: Which dimension/measurement is the unknown (V, SA, l, w, h, edge)
Specific integer values
Whether the stem uses 'rectangular prism', 'box', 'cube', or 'right square prism' wording
common_bugs: Cloner converts SA formula incorrectly when one face is missing (forgets that 5 s^2 vs 6 s^2 changes both the answer and the key)
Hard SA-from-V chain produces a non-integer edge, breaking the integer assumption
Symbolic-SA clone drops or duplicates a 2 in 2(lw+lh+wh), making the expression algebraically wrong

types.name: Cylinder volume or dimension
types.description: A right circular cylinder is given by radius (or diameter or circumference of base) and height; the student computes V=pi*r^2*h or reverses to find r, h, or the base area.
types.core_skill_demand: Recall V=pi*r^2*h, substitute, and either multiply (forward) or divide (reverse). At Hard, an extra step converts circumference/diameter to radius first, or expresses V symbolically.

characteristics: Not represented at Easy in this bank.
Radius (or diameter, requiring one halving step) and height both given as small integers
Answer expressed in 'k pi' form
Reverse variant: given V (and base area or one dimension) find the other -> one division step
medium.example_stem: A right circular cylinder has height 8 m and base radius 12 m. What is the volume, in cubic meters?

characteristics: Radius given only indirectly via circumference -> r = C / (2 pi) first, then V
Symbolic answer: V expressed as a function of r where h is given in terms of r
Reverse with non-trivial algebra (V given, height given, solve for r requiring a square root)
hard.example_stem: The circumference of the base of a cylinder is 20 pi m and the height is 6 m. What is the volume?

must_preserve: Right circular cylinder (no oblique, no truncated)
Exact V=pi*r^2*h application; pi remains symbolic in the answer when the stem requests 'k pi'
Difficulty signal: Medium requires direct r/h; Hard requires a conversion or symbolic expression step
must_vary: Which of r, d, C, h, V is the unknown
Integer values for r and h
Whether the answer is in 'k pi' or numeric form
common_bugs: Cloner forgets to halve a diameter, producing a wrong key by factor 4
Hard clone collapses circumference -> radius into a single step, removing the conversion that defines Hard
Distractors fail to mirror the classic r-vs-d confusion, removing the obvious trap

types.name: Cone / sphere / hemisphere volume
types.description: A right circular cone, sphere, or hemisphere is given by radius, diameter, or height; the student applies V=(1/3)pi*r^2*h or V=(4/3)pi*r^3 (or one-half of it for a hemisphere), forward or in reverse.
types.core_skill_demand: Recall the (1/3) factor for a cone or (4/3) factor for a sphere and substitute. At Hard, the formula is rearranged (e.g. given cone volume find slant height via Pythagorean theorem with the base radius).

characteristics: Not represented at Easy.
Sphere or cone with radius given as a small integer or simple fraction
Answer remains in terms of pi
Single application of the formula
medium.example_stem: A sphere has a radius of 17/5 feet. What is the volume, in cubic feet?

characteristics: Reverse: cone V and base radius (or base area) given -> find height -> then use Pythagorean theorem to find slant height
OR hemisphere where the (1/2) factor on V_sphere must be remembered and result is approximated
Multi-step chain (extract r from base area, plug into V, possibly approximate)
hard.example_stem: A right circular cone has volume 71,148 pi cubic cm and base area 5,929 pi sq cm. What is the slant height?

must_preserve: Solid type (cone, sphere, or hemisphere) and the matching (1/3), (4/3), or (2/3) factor
At Hard, the chain length: cone Hard must end with slant height via Pythagoras OR approximation step
Hemisphere clones must preserve the half-of-sphere semantics
must_vary: Specific radius / height numbers
Whether the stem provides base area or base radius
Whether the answer is exact (k pi) or an approximation
common_bugs: Cloner forgets (1/3) for the cone, producing distractors that look like cylinder answers
Hemisphere clone divides by 2 the diameter instead of the volume (or vice versa)
Hard cone clone skips the Pythagorean step, collapsing it to a Medium 'find h from V' question

aspects.name: Similar figures and scaling
aspects.description: Student is told that two figures are similar (or that one's linear dimensions are a constant multiple of the other's) and must propagate that linear scale factor k to a perimeter (factor k), area (factor k^2), surface area (factor k^2), or volume (factor k^3). The cognitive operation is recognizing the dimension of the measured quantity and raising the linear scale to the matching power.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A3 differs from A1/A2 because a clone of A3 must compare two figures via a scale factor — turning the question into a single-figure 'compute the area' problem is categorical drift. A3 differs from A4 because the comparison is between abstract similar primitives (squares, rectangles, triangles, prisms), not a real-world scale model embedded in a context.

types.name: Similar 2D figures: side -> area scaling
types.description: Two similar 2D figures (squares, rectangles, parallelograms, triangles) are given with a linear scale factor (or with one area and the linear scale); the student must apply area_ratio = k^2.
types.core_skill_demand: Recognize that scaling all linear dimensions by k multiplies the area by k^2; apply forward (k known, areas to compare) or reverse (areas known, find k or a side).

characteristics: Not represented at Easy.
Linear scale factor k given explicitly as a small positive integer (e.g. 6 times, 246 times)
Single application: given side ratio, square it for area ratio
Forward direction only (no reverse algebra)
medium.example_stem: Square A has side lengths 246 times those of square B. The area of A is k times that of B. What is k?

characteristics: Scale factor must be inferred from a ratio of areas (sqrt step) before propagating to a side or perimeter
OR scale factor is given as a percentage increase (e.g. 20%) and must be converted to 1.2 before squaring
OR scale factor is fractional (1/10) and squared to a small fraction
Reverse direction: given two areas and one side, find the corresponding side in the other figure
hard.example_stem: Rectangle ABCD has area 648 sq in and rectangle EFGH (similar) has area 72 sq in. ABCD's longest side is 36 in. What is the longest side of EFGH?

must_preserve: Two similar figures explicitly named (not a single figure)
The k^2 relationship between linear scale and area scale must remain the central operation
Difficulty signal: Medium clones give k directly; Hard clones require either sqrt-of-area-ratio, percentage conversion, or reverse algebra
must_vary: Whether figures are squares, rectangles, parallelograms, or triangles
The numerical scale factor (integer, fraction, percentage)
Direction (forward area or reverse side)
common_bugs: Cloner applies linear scale to area without squaring (categorical error — produces wrong key)
Hard clone replaces a fractional or percentage k with an integer k, collapsing to Medium
Reverse clone yields a non-square-rational area ratio, making the sqrt step irrational and the key non-integer

types.name: Similar 3D solids: side -> volume / SA scaling
types.description: Two similar 3D solids (typically right rectangular prisms or cylinders) are described; the student must propagate the linear scale factor to volume (k^3) or surface area (k^2), often chaining: SA_ratio -> k -> V_ratio.
types.core_skill_demand: Recognize that SA_ratio=k^2 and V_ratio=k^3; chain the two relations to convert between linear, surface, and volume ratios; OR find the scale factor that produces a specified volume multiplier (e.g. 392x).

characteristics: Not represented.
Not represented at Medium for 3D similarity in this bank.
Two solids with surface areas given; student must take sqrt(SA_ratio)=k and then cube it to scale V
OR find an integer pair (R/r, H/h) whose product r^2*h scales V by a target multiplier (e.g. 392=8^2*7^1)
OR a composite scaling: two identical prisms glued, requiring deriving k from a surface-area ratio K -> (92/47)K
hard.example_stem: Right rectangular prism X is similar to Y. SA of X is 58 sq cm, SA of Y is 1,450 sq cm. Volume of Y is 1,250 cu cm. What is the volume of X?

must_preserve: Two 3D solids (or one solid scaled twice) — never collapse to a single computation on one solid
Both k^2 and k^3 relations must be operative in the solution chain
Numerical setup such that sqrt(SA_ratio) and cube root, or the chosen factorization, yield clean integers
must_vary: Type of solid (rectangular prism, cylinder, cone)
Which ratio is the unknown (V from SA, R/r given V multiplier, etc.)
Whether the scale factor is given numerically or must be inferred
common_bugs: Cloner mixes up which exponent applies to which measure (uses k^2 for V or k^3 for SA)
Numerical scaling chosen so that sqrt(SA_ratio) is irrational — breaks the clean key
Scale-factor-search variant (e.g. 392x volume) chosen with target number that has no integer factorization a^2 * b

types.name: Similar triangles / polygons: perimeter or corresponding side ratio
types.description: Two similar polygons (usually triangles) are given; the student uses the linear scale factor to find a perimeter, a corresponding side, or to scale an area at a still-linear level (e.g. ratio of areas of similar right triangles via leg ratio).
types.core_skill_demand: Recognize that all corresponding linear measures share the same scale factor k (perimeter, side); compute k from one pair of corresponding sides or perimeters, then apply.

characteristics: Linear scale factor k is given or trivially derivable from one pair of corresponding sides or two perimeters
Only one linear measure (perimeter or side) is asked
Small integer arithmetic; no area-vs-side dimension mix
easy.example_stem: Triangle ABC and DEF are similar; corresponding sides given; perimeter of ABC is 20. Perimeter of DEF?

characteristics: Scale factor must be computed from a table or ratio of two perimeters before applying to find a side
Includes one extra abstraction step (e.g. perimeter ratio = side ratio implicit)
medium.example_stem: Table gives perimeters of similar triangles TUV (37) and XYZ; TU=18. Find XY.

characteristics: Not represented at Hard for linear-only triangle similarity; Hard variants of triangle similarity migrate into A3.T1 (area scaling).

must_preserve: Pair of similar triangles (or polygons) named in the stem
Asked quantity is a linear measure (side or perimeter) so the scale factor is applied to the first power
Clean integer arithmetic for the scale factor
must_vary: Whether scale factor is inferred from sides or from perimeters
Direction (find perimeter of bigger triangle given smaller, or vice versa)
Triangle labeling and numerical sides
common_bugs: Cloner applies k^2 to a perimeter, drifting into A3.T1
Distractors don't include the classic 'used the wrong direction of k' error
Clones a side-from-perimeter setup but forgets perimeter ratio = side ratio

aspects.name: Composite, coordinate, and contextual figures
aspects.description: Student must first construct, decompose, or interpret a figure before any area/volume formula can be applied: combine two primitives (sum of areas), subtract one from another, compute area from coordinate points, or extract dimensions from a real-world context (scale model, manufactured container, glass shape).
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A4 differs from A1 and A2 because the dimensions used in the formula are not given directly — they must be assembled by adding/subtracting two figures, by computing distances from coordinates, or by translating a verbal context. A4 differs from A3 because A4 does not compare two similar figures via a scale factor; even when a 'scale model' appears (e.g. 9fec9d49), the operation is one-shot area scaling and is therefore classified to A3.T1 — A4 instead handles composites, coordinate-defined figures, and engineering/word-problem contexts.

types.name: Composite / subtractive 2D and 3D figures
types.description: Two or more figures are combined (sum) or one figure removed from another (difference). Student computes each piece's area or volume and combines them.
types.core_skill_demand: Compute area or volume of each component using A1/A2 formulas, then add or subtract; recognize which formula applies to each component.

characteristics: Both components are 2D primitives with explicit area/dimension given in the stem (no separate formula step needed for one of them)
One add or one subtract step; integer arithmetic
Both shapes are named (rectangle, triangle, square)
easy.example_stem: Triangle R has area 80 cm^2; square S has side 4 cm. What is the total area of R and S?

characteristics: Both components require their own area calculation from dimensions; the comparison or difference is then taken
May include figure interpretation (a labeled diagram with two named rectangles)
medium.example_stem: Rectangle ABCD has AB=12; rectangle QRST has QR=5 and QT=10. What is the difference of their areas (or similar)?

characteristics: 3D composite: one solid inscribed in / removed from another (e.g. sphere inside cube)
Requires two full 3D formulas (V_cube and V_sphere) and a final rounding/approximation step
Large numerical values (e.g. edge 68 in)
hard.example_stem: A cube has edge 68 in; a sphere of radius 34 in is inside, touching each face's center. What is the volume of the space in the cube not taken by the sphere?

must_preserve: Two clearly named figures combined or subtracted
The combine/subtract operation remains the structural hinge of the question
At Hard, the inscribed-solid geometry is exact (e.g. sphere diameter equals cube edge)
must_vary: Which shapes are combined (triangle+square, rect+rect, cube-sphere, cube-cylinder, etc.)
Whether the operation is sum or difference
Specific numerical dimensions
common_bugs: Cloner forgets the inscribed-solid constraint (sphere diameter = cube edge), making the figure geometrically impossible
Distractors no longer include the 'forgot to subtract' or 'added instead of subtracted' classic errors
Cloner picks Hard numbers that don't round cleanly, producing a key that disagrees with all distractors

types.name: Coordinate-defined figure: area or circle
types.description: Three (or more) points are given by coordinates and define a triangle (whose area must be found) or a circle (whose circumference / radius must be found via the right angle inscribed in a semicircle, or via the distance formula).
types.core_skill_demand: Use the distance formula or coordinate-based area formulas (e.g. (1/2)|x1(y2-y3)+...|) to extract the required lengths, then apply the area or circle formula.

characteristics: Not represented at Easy.
Coordinates produce axis-aligned distances (one leg is vertical, one horizontal) so the lengths are read off directly
Figure is a 'nice' shape (right triangle, square with diagonals on axes)
Single distance-formula step (or none) and one formula application
medium.example_stem: Three points (4,8), (7,11), (7,5) define a circle; circumference is k*pi. Find k.

characteristics: Coordinates yield non-axis-aligned segments so the distance formula (with sqrt) is required twice
Triangle area requires the determinant-style coordinate formula, not just (1/2) bh on visible legs
Larger or more spread-out coordinates
hard.example_stem: Triangle with vertices (-3,4), (4,-3), (5,3). What is its area, in square units?

must_preserve: Figure defined purely by coordinate points
Required answer is area, circumference, radius, or another standard A1/A2 quantity
At Hard, at least one side must be non-axis-aligned to require the distance formula
must_vary: Specific coordinates (rotated, reflected, translated)
Whether the figure is triangle, square, or circle
Asked quantity (area vs circumference vs radius)
common_bugs: Cloner picks coordinates that yield ugly sqrt values, making the key irrational
Coordinate triangle clone uses axis-aligned points at Hard, collapsing to Medium

Circle-from-three-points clone forgets that the right-angle inscribed in a semicircle condition fixes the diameter (when applicable), so the chosen points don't actually lie on a single small-radius circle

types.name: Real-world / contextual prism or cylinder
types.description: A real-world object (drinking glass, fruit can, manufactured cylinder, ballroom floor) is described; the student must extract the relevant geometric primitive's dimensions from the context and apply a volume or area formula, sometimes including approximation or unit conversion.
types.core_skill_demand: Translate a verbal description (and possibly a figure) into the canonical primitive, identify which dimension is unknown, and apply the appropriate 3D formula (often cylinder V) — usually with one extra step like percentage adjustment or subtraction of a known volume.

characteristics: Not represented at Easy.
Real-world object described as a single named primitive (cylinder, prism)
Exactly one extra translation step: percentage increase in a dimension, or subtraction of a known partial volume, or unit conversion (cm <-> fluid ounces) given in the stem
Numerical setup yields a numeric answer that can be matched to MCQ options
medium.example_stem: Cylindrical containers: container A has radius 16 cm; container B has radius 25% longer than A. Find the volume of B.

characteristics: Not represented at Hard for contextual prisms in this bank; Hard real-world objects tend to migrate into A2.T2 / A2.T3 (cylinder V from circumference, cone slant).

must_preserve: Real-world surface description that maps unambiguously to one canonical primitive
Single 'translate-and-add' step beyond the standard formula (e.g. percentage adjustment, partial-volume subtraction)
Sensible physical dimensions (no negative radii, no implausible heights)
must_vary: The narrative object (can, glass, tank, model, floor)
Which dimension is given vs unknown
The extra step (percentage, subtraction, conversion)
common_bugs: Cloner removes the contextual step, collapsing the question to a plain A2.T2
Clone uses a percentage that doesn't yield a clean radius (e.g. 33% increase on 16 -> ugly product)
Distractors don't reflect classic 'forgot to subtract syrup volume' or 'percent applied to volume instead of radius' errors

skill_wide_notes: Most Easy items in this bank live in A1.T1 (rectangle/square area-perimeter) or A2.T1 (rectangular prism volume). Medium items concentrate in A2.T2 (cylinders) and A1.T3 (circles). Hard items cluster in A2 (3D solids with formula chains: SA <-> V, circumference -> r, slant height) and A3 (similar-figure scaling, especially k^2 and k^3 propagation). A3 is the most common source of categorical drift in clones: a Hard similar-figure question silently rewritten without the second figure becomes an A1/A2 single-figure clone. A4 questions almost always involve one extra non-formula step (subtract, combine, distance-formula, percentage); preserving that step is essential for difficulty fidelity.
